coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on nitrogen-decorated carbon dots for Listeria monocytogenes determination using a screen-printed carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Jampasa, Sakda
Ngamrojanavanich, Nattaya
Rengpipat, Sirirat
Chailapakul, Orawon
Kalcher, Kurt
Chaiyo, Sudkate
coredata.dc:description: Current method for identification of foodborne pathogens suffers from its relatively poor performance, consequently limiting its use. Herein, we first describe an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor based on nitrogen-decorated carbon dots (NCDs) for Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) determination using a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE). Citric acid serves as carbon source, and ethylenediamine, a molecule containing nitrogen atom, is employed to synthesize CDs. Approximately 4 nm NCD with homogenous size distribution can be produced via a single step green microwave-assisted methodology. The construction of ECL sensor is initiated by the immobilization of capture antibody (Ab1) onto the carboxyl graphene (GOOH)-modified SPCE, where immunocomplexes (antigen and the NCD-labelled secondary antibody (Ab2-NCD)) are formed, resulting in a substantial increment in the ECL signal response in the presence of K2S2O8. The GOOH allows direct formation of the capture antibodies and enhances the electrochemical properties. Under optimal parameters, this sensor exhibits wide linearity (2 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1), high sensitivity (0.104 or 1.0 × 10−1 CFU mL−1) and specificity over the nontargeting studied pathogens and is successfully applied to determine L. monocytogenes in food products. These promising results together with its performance suggest that this proposed platform may serve as an alternative device to effectively control the spread of foodborne diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence sensor
Nitrogen-decorated carbon dots
A green methodology
Listeria monocytogenes
Screen-printed carbon electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL immunosensor fabrication and determination of L. monocytogenes
Characterization and electrochemiluminescence behavior of the synthesized NCDs
Verification of the successfully modified ECL sensor
Electrochemiluminescence detection of L. monocytogenes using developed ECL sensor
Experimental optimization of the ECL immunosensor
Quantification of target analyte employing the established ECL sensing platform
Reproducibility and stability of the developed sensor
Listeria monocytogenes determination in food sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra and (B) SEM images of the SPCE and GOOH-SPCE. (C) Cyclic voltammograms and (D) Nyquist impedance comparison of the SPCE, GOOH/SPCE, Ab1/GOOH/SPCE, An/Ab1/GOOH/SPCE and Ab2/An/Ab1/GOOH/SPCE. The experiment was conducted by using 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- dissolved in 0.1 M KCl solution and scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

(A) ECL–time curve of the ECL immunosensor; The concentrations of L. monocytogenes were from 0 to 106 CFU mL−1. (B) Overall calibration plot of L. monocytogenes versus ΔECL intensity response, and (B, inset) the linear calibration plot of the logarithm of L. monocytogenes concentration and the ΔECL intensity (from 2 to 106 CFU mL−1).
Illustration of (A) the NCD-conjugated secondary antibodies and (B) the construction and detection of the developed ECL sensor for L. monocytogenes.

(A) ECL signal illustration of the Ab1/GOOH/SPCE without (a) and with (b) 0.1 M K2S2O8 in the absence of target analyte and by using the different concentrations (c and d) of L. monocytogenes (10 and 500 CFU mL−1). Potential curve comparison (inset) of the Ab1/GOOH/SPCE electrode at the similarly specified conditions as observed by ECL. (B) The feasible mechanism of NCD in the existence of K2S2O8 at the transducer surface.
Comparison of different methods for L. monocytogenes determination.
Comparison of the obtained results between the proposed ECL sensor and the standard method for L. monocytogenes in different food samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on nitrogen-decorated carbon dots for Listeria monocytogenes determination using a screen-printed carbon electrode

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Biotechnology and Genetic Engineering, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biotechnology and Genetic Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current method for identification of foodborne pathogens suffers from its relatively poor performance, consequently limiting its use. Herein, we first describe an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor based on nitrogen-decorated carbon dots (NCDs) for Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) determination using a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE). Citric acid serves as carbon source, and ethylenediamine, a molecule containing nitrogen atom, is employed to synthesize CDs. Approximately 4 nm NCD with homogenous size distribution can be produced via a single step green microwave-assisted methodology. The construction of ECL sensor is initiated by the immobilization of capture antibody (Ab1) onto the carboxyl graphene (GOOH)-modified SPCE, where immunocomplexes (antigen and the NCD-labelled secondary antibody (Ab2-NCD)) are formed, resulting in a substantial increment in the ECL signal response in the presence of K2S2O8. The GOOH allows direct formation of the capture antibodies and enhances the electrochemical properties. Under optimal parameters, this sensor exhibits wide linearity (2 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1), high sensitivity (0.104 or 1.0 × 10−1 CFU mL−1) and specificity over the nontargeting studied pathogens and is successfully applied to determine L. monocytogenes in food products. These promising results together with its performance suggest that this proposed platform may serve as an alternative device to effectively control the spread of foodborne diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases are a major issue of food safety typically caused by contamination of pathogenic bacteria, viruses, and parasites (Bintsis, 2017; Zhao et al., 2014). This problem has grown to become a worldwide public health concern, especially in developing countries where agriculture is the heart of their economy (Oliver et al., 2005). Yet, the number of global incidences of foodborne disease is not accurately accounted due to the current low performance detection method which complicates the monitoring and controlling the spread of the diseases (Hirvonen et al., 2012; Priyanka et al., 2016). Due to this mentioned situation, research and development should help advocate for food safety awareness.

Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), a foodborne pathogen and the gram-positive bacteria, has been involved as the causative organisms in numerous outbreaks of foodborne diseases. (Farber and Peterkin, 1991). It has been recently confirmed that most of these people: pregnant women, infants and adults with weakened immune systems, and people aged 65 or older are high-risk groups that can become easily infected by these harmful bacteria. According to World Health Organization (WHO), even though the number of cases caused by L. monocytogenes is moderately low, but the high mortality rate (~16.25%) poses an important public health concern among other cases (de Noordhout et al., 2014). There is an extreme necessity for early detection of this pathogen.

In recent times, electrochemiluminescence (ECL), cell culture, enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) and oligonucleotide-based sensors have been successfully established for the detection of L. monocytogenes in food industry (Liu et al., 2021a; Qi et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2014). Nonetheless, detection sensitivity of the first-three mentioned strategies is inadequate to determine trace levels of L. monocytogenes, and therefore limits their practicality. This is the most challenging aspect for L. monocytogenes determination as these bacteria are capable of rapidly growing and reproducing at ambient conditions and widely circulate within commercial food products and drinks, resulting in uncontrollable outbreaks of the foodborne diseases (Shamloo et al., 2019). For the last oligonucleotide-based sensor, multiple steps and high-cost reagents are required. To overcome these limitations, developing a highly sensitive, cost-effective, facile, and precise technique for the determination of L. monocytogenes is greatly required as it can possibly prevent infection and distribution of the foodborne diseases globally.

Considering the advantages of the earlier developed methods, ECL sensor has gained substantial consideration for the invention of an ultrasensitive approach for L. monocytogenes determination. This technique integrates the benefits of electrochemical measurement and chemiluminescence strategy, such as affordable price, uncomplicated instrumentation, low background current and high sensitivity and specificity (Gross et al., 2016). As these mentioned advantages, ECL has been then applied as a detection method in numerous areas (Hu and Xu, 2010; Qin et al., 2020). Generally, there are two important components in the ECL-resonance energy transfer (RET) process: coreactant and ECL initiation element. Coreactants play an important role in the reductive–oxidative mechanism in the ECL process, in which an intermediate with ionic radical is first created by applying potential with a constant scan rate. The intermediates in system will then interact together and release ECL signal, which will be eventually amplified in the final step. The sensing capability of ECL can be improved by doping simply with precious nanomaterials or molecules containing heterogenous atoms (Edison et al., 2016; Qin et al., 2020).

In previous reports, ruthenium, quantum dot (QD) or QD loaded with nanoparticles is employed as an ECL signal initiator in order to allow the sensor to be electrochemically detectable (Du et al., 2019; Nichkova et al., 2007; Qin et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2009). The expensive implemented materials are a major drawback in addition to its toxicity which further requires the separation and elimination. Hence, a non-toxic, low-cost, and highly sensitive labeling element is essential for the fabrication of an ECL sensor. Carbon dot (CD), a newly developed carbon-based material owing to the similar stellar characteristics of QD, has received tremendous research interest in various applications, such as molecular imaging and in vivo molecular tracking (Jiao et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020). CDs exhibit high photostability, constantly nonblinking fluorescence signal and simply tunable emission. CD has an excellent water solubility and is economically synthesized by a green microwave-assisted method (Edison et al., 2016). CDs contain no heavy metal, which could make them more environmentally friendly and much safer for immunosensing applications (Yang et al., 2009). Because of these excellent properties, CD has been therefore used in fabricating numerous electrochemiluminescence biosensors.

In this work, we describe the green methodology based on the nitrogen-decorated CDs (NCDs) as an electrochemiluminescence initiator integrated with a disposable screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for an ultrasensitive determination of L. monocytogenes in food products. Nitrogen heterogenous atom is chemically doped to enlarge the CD electrochemiluminescence behaviors. N atom offers compatible chemistry for bonding with carbon atom, which hugely enhances desirable characteristics of the modified CD such as an improved ECL intensity (Du et al., 2019). This combination further exhibits no cytotoxicity, making it extremely suitable to be applied in this work. The NCDs can also be prepared easily by using a single step green microwave-assisted method. Citric acid and ethylenediamine serve as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. A sandwich-type format, one of the most popular categories in immunoassay, is implemented for the construction of L. monocytogenes sensor (Qin et al., 2020). Carboxyl graphene (GOOH) is used as the electrode modifier to directly introduce the assigned functionality and to increase the electrical conductivity of this proposed platform. ECL sensor exhibits a remarkable increase in ECL signal response in the presence of target analyte, whereas it is scarcely observed in the absence thereof, signifying its effective monitoring of L. monocytogenes. ECL signal gradually monotonically rises with further increase in L. monocytogenes concentration. Using this proposed platform, the improved detection sensitivity, specificity, selectivity, and stability are achieved. This strategy has enormous potential for early identifying and detecting a trace level of L. monocytogenes, which could strongly impact medical diagnostics and could widen its applications in numerous fields.
All chemicals, reagents and apparatuses are presented in Supplementary Information (SI), section 1.1.
Details and production of specific antibodies against L. monocytogenes can be found in SI, section 1.2.

Ethylenediamine and citric acid were used as the nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively, to produce NCD as they are commercially available. A microwave was employed to generate a controllable heat in the CD synthesizing step (Edison et al., 2016). Briefly, 1.0 g critic acid was dissolved with 10 mL MQ and subsequently mixed with 0.4 mL ethylenediamine under the specified reaction conditions: 270 °C temperature, 0 psi pressure and 300 w power for 3 min in the microwave. The black powder NCD product obtained was approximately 4 nm (see more details in SI, section 1.4). The obtained solid product was washed with a large amount of water and dried using a freeze dry machine for 24 h. This finished CD was preserved in a desiccator for further labeling step.

Conjugation of the NCD and Ab2 can be straightforwardly performed by using an EDC/NHS standard reaction (Fischer, 2010; Jampasa et al., 2018). NCDs (1 mg mL−1, 700 μL) having numerous active sites of a carboxylate group (–COOH) were initially activated with mixture solution of a 20 mM EDC/NHS (100 μL) for 1 h at an ambient temperature (RT, 25 °C). The NCD with carboxylate functional group (–COOH) was then transformed into an amine-reactive ester (–COONHS) after this step, which is ready to react with the primary amino groups (–NH2) on the Ab2. This activated NCD was mixed with the Ab2 (5 mg mL−1, 200 μL) under gently stirring condition for 24 h at 4 °C to allow covalent conjugation. NCD-labelled Ab2 was subsequently dialyzed in 0.01 mol L−1 PBS of pH 7.4 at 4 °C for 48 h to eradicate the unreacted molecules surplus, such as NCD, EDC or NHS. These purified fractions were kept at −20 °C until use. The procedure of this conjugation step is illustrated in Scheme 1 A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL immunosensor fabrication and determination of L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: SPCE was used as a transducer in this work for its specific properties and cheap fabrication, as previously described (Yamanaka et al., 2016). The construction of ECL immunosensor began with the modification of the SPCE surface with GOOH. A 0.1 mg mL−1 of an aqueous-based GOOH solution (5 μL) was dropped onto the SPCE surface and left to dry for 30 min at RT, 25 °C. To further conduct the next immobilization step, surface chemistry of the GOOH-modified SPCE was activated with EDC/NHS (20 mM, 20 μL) for 1 h at RT. The covalent immobilization of the Ab1 (500 μg mL−1) was followed using the following conditions: 5 μL the Ab1, 24 h at 4 °C. The Ab1-modified SPCE was thoroughly washed twice with PBS of pH 7.4 in order to remove the Ab1 and excess EDC/NHS. After this cleaning step, the blocking process was adopted by casting a 0.1% BSA (5 μL) onto the electrode surface for 1 h and preserved at 4 °C when prior to use. The blocking step was purposely adopted for reducing non-specific adsorption during incubation and detection steps. This adsorption could eventually account for poor sensitivity and specificity of the fabricated sensor.

For the determination step, the prepared Ab1/GOOH/SPCE sensor was incubated with L. monocytogenes or practical sample containing target analyte (100 μL) for 1 h at RT, followed by the conjugation of the NCD-labelled Ab2 (20 μL) for 1 h to develop immunocomplexes. The NCD-labelled Ab2/An/Ab1/GOOH/SPCE was then transferred to a dark sealed box that was custom-designed to have a fixed interior PMT lamp. After the addition of K2S2O8 coreactant (0.1 M in PBS pH 8.5, 20 μL), the initial potential was then scanned from 0 to −1.4 V with a scanning rate of 200 mV s−1 by utilizing CV. During this scanning process, the PMT lamp was supplied with an 800 kV from a power supplier and the ECL responsive signal was recorded with an e-coder machine. The presence of L. monocytogenes can be monitored by an improvement in an ECL emission signal, in which the signal will increase with increasing L. monocytogenes. It is noted that the washing step was always performed after completing each modifying step using PBS of pH 7.4. The standard curve of L. monocytogenes was studied over a concentration ranging from 2.0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1. The fabrication and determination protocols are summarized in Scheme 1B.

To evaluate analytical performance of the developed ECL sensor, limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated by using the constructed linear regression according to the following equations: 3SDb/S and 10SDb/S, respectively, where SDb is the standard deviation of a sample without target analyte (n = 10), and S is the slope of the linear graph.
Details and sample preparation protocols are provided in SI, section 1.3.

Emission signal of an electrochemiluminescence element can be adjusted by either chemical or physical modification (Liu et al., 2021b). Generally, an element's ability to emit signal significantly affects the detection sensitivity for analysis, thereby it is necessary to enlarge this aspect to meet these criteria. In previous reports, silver nanoparticles, ruthenium, gold nanoparticles or nitrogen atom, was employed as materials to alter the designed property of the produced CD or QD (Li et al., 2019; Nozaki et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2009). However, among these materials, a molecule containing plenty number of nitrogen atoms are extensively used as it possesses not only the ability to enlarge the ECL signal but also provides more active sites to allow more biomolecules to chemically or physically adhere, resulting in an amplified signal (Kou et al., 2020). Besides, the use of N atoms is biocompatible and non-cytotoxic. In this work, citric acid served as carbon source, and ethylenediamine was assigned as nitrogen source to produce CD with one main purpose: to improve ECL emission signal and lower detection limit. In addition, GOOH was utilized as the electrode modifier to directly attach the primary capture antibodies onto the electrode surface. GOOH employed additionally possessed high electrical conductivity, which could also tremendously improve the detection sensitivity of ECL sensor.

Not only the materials applied are superior, but the procedures we used are environmentally friendly. Microwave was implemented as a heat generator to synthesize CD as it is low-cost, easy to use, controllable and portable. These advantages altogether allow a narrow size distribution and precise chemistry of the produced CD. These materials and methodologies applied unquestionably make this proposed platform a unique alternative to the earlier reports or conventional approaches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and electrochemiluminescence behavior of the synthesized NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A set of techniques such as TEM, UV–vis and FTIR, was used to verify the successful production of NCD. The results and discussion for each characterization technique can be found in SI, Fig. S1, section 1.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the successfully modified ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the success of the constructed ECL sensor, confirmation in a step-by-step fashion is required. SPCE was initially casted with GOOH to directly introduce the carboxylate groups to the electrode surface, and then used for the further immobilization process. Fig. 1 A compares FTIR spectra of both the unmodified and modified SPCEs. All the GOOH characteristic bands were present at the GOOH-modified SPCE (b, red line), including CO stretching at 1775 cm−1, C–O stretching at 1073 cm−1, and the OH stretching at 3219 cm−1 (Edison et al., 2016). On the contrary, FTIR spectrum of the unmodified SPCE exhibited no significant peaks, verifying the absence of GOOH on its surface (a, black line). Moreover, physical surface morphologies of the GOOH-SPCE and SPCE were compared. As seen in Fig. 1B, SEM images distinguished the presence and absence of GOOH casted on the electrode surface. The GOOH-modified SPCE displayed a thin layer and crumbled sheet on the surface (b. GOOH/SPCE); meanwhile, a smoother surface with no GOOH sheet was observed at the unmodified SPCE (a. SPCE). These results indicated the successful modification of SPCE.

Besides, the fabricated ECL sensor was electrochemically characterized employing CV and EIS. Both CV and EIS approaches showed results attained from each characterization step (Figs. 1C and D). As seen from Fig. 1C, the current response of the redox probe significantly increased after modifying with GOOH (b. COOH/SPCE, red line). This increment could be explained by the incorporation of GOOH that owns a better electrochemical property than the unmodified SPCE (a. SPCE, black line) (Smith et al., 2019). Afterward, the current response began to decrease after more modifying steps were performed (c, d, and e). These results were from the presence of antibodies, proteins or immunocomplexes that possess more insulation property on the electrode surface (Feng et al., 2013). The formation of these immunocomplexes generate not only the insulation barrier but also possess plenty of the negative charges. The number of the negative charges distributing on the electrode surface may hugely impact on the redox probe accessibility (Boonkaew et al., 2019). As a result, a gradual decrease in the current response was observed. For EIS study, semicircle reflects charge transfer resistance of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox couple at electrode interface. The obtained EIS results were consistent with CV (Fig. 1D, a-e), which strongly supported the successful alteration of the ECL sensor. The EIS characteristics can be elucidated by the similar effects as discussed in CV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemiluminescence detection of L. monocytogenes using developed ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore an electrochemiluminescence behavior of the constructed sensor, the ECL signal response was recorded and compared with and without the target analyte. It is assumed that the higher concentration of target analyte exists, the greater number of immunocomplexes can be accomplished. K2S2O8 was employed as a coreactant in this work because of its high-ECL signal response, inexpensiveness, greater solubility, and less poisonousness (Li et al., 2019). ECL intensity and potential curve were first investigated in the absence of L. monocytogenes. As displayed in Scheme 2 A, ECL signal response (a and b) and potential curve (a and b, inset) were scarcely detected for all cases. Yet, a negligible signal at the Ab1/GOOH/SPCE was still observed. This small intense signal signified non-specific adsorption of the added components (coreactant or NCD-labelled Ab2) on the electrode surface. However, ECL intensity (c) and potential curve (c, inset) remarkably increased after addition of the target analyte. These detected signals further improved as the target L. monocytogenes concentration increased (d and d inset). In addition, ECL characteristics of the nitrogen-modified (Fig. S2B) and unmodified (Fig. S2A) CD were compared, and it was revealed that the NCD sensor produced higher ECL signal response compared with the unmodified CD nearly 5 times (Fig. S2C). These results proved that the enhanced ECL signal is initiated from the decorated nitrogen. All ECL result plots against L. monocytogenes concentrations will be background subtracted to achieve a certain signal for quantitative analysis. All these things signified the performance of the developed ECL sensor for effective and sensitive monitoring of L. monocytogenes. In addition, a crucial involvement of the NCD and K2S2O8 coreactant in the ECL-RET process was discussed.

In a recent work, the feasible mechanism of ECL initiation of NCD in the presence of K2S2O8 was described (Li et al., 2019). Immunocomplexes composing of NCD-labelled antibodies, antigens and capture antibodies were first created on the electrode surface. Subsequent the electrochemical reduction of NCDs was performed to generate NCD•‾ intermediate and to reduce the added K2S2O8 to lastly give SO4 •‾ simultaneously. These two products underwent reaction to form the excited state NCDs*. Then, the NCDs* transferred its energy to NCDs in the final process and subsequently released cathodic optical signal, which is proportional on L. monocytogenes concentration (Scheme 2A). These results indicated that the NCD and K2S2O8 coreactant are the components required to succeed the ECL-RET reaction. The ECL mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental optimization of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Developing an approach capable of sensitively detecting L. monocytogenes is a key requirement in this work. Therefore, variable parameters influencing the performance of the proposed platform such as the concentration of GOOH, the primary antibody concentration, the incubation time, and the concentration of coreactant, as well as the effect of electrolyte pH were concisely investigated. For parameters accessed, the results revealed that the optimum conditions were 0.1 mg mL−1 of GOOH, 500 μg mL−1 of Ab1, 60 min of incubation time, 0.1 M of coreactant, and pH 8 of electrolyte, respectively. All data and its interpretation are provided in SI, Fig. S3, section 1.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of target analyte employing the established ECL sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical performance of this ECL sensing platform was assessed by using a sample solution containing various concentrations of L. monocytogenes target (from 0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1). Under the optimal conditions, the constructed sensor displayed a sensitive response to the presence of target analyte (Figs. 2 A and B). The ECL responsive signal increased with the logarithm of L. monocytogenes in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 2B, inset). Using this proposed platform, the linear concentration ranging from 2.0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1 was established. Furthermore, the corresponding linear equation (y = 34.848Log L. monocytogenes – 37.086) with an acceptable R
2 (0.998) was acquired. From the linear response, the limit of detection (LOD) (3SDblank/slope) and limit of quantification (LOQ) (10SDblank/slope) were found to be 0.104 (1.0 × 10−1) CFU•mL−1 and 0.345 (3.4 × 10−1) CFU•mL−1, respectively.

As tabulated in Table 1 , the LOQ achieved from this developed ECL sensor was lower than that of the values reported by the previous methods. In addition, this ECL sensor further exhibited wider linear range than the recent approaches, demonstrating its excellent analytical performance. This developed ECL sensor is competitive with other related L. monocytogenes sensing platforms in terms of sensitivity, simplicity, and operational cost, which make it immensely suitable and alternative for L. monocytogenes analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility and stability of the created platform were estimated in this section. These aspects were expressed in percent relative standard deviation (%RSD). The reproducibility was explored using various concentrations of target analyte (1.0 × 101, 1.0× 102 and 1.0 × 103 CFU mL−1) (n = 10), and it was found that the %RSDs in the interval range from 4.05 to 5.98 were obtained (Figs. S4A and SI). In accordance with AOAC manual program, these achieved %RSDs were acceptable. For another aspect, the finished sensors were kept at 4 °C in refrigerator until use. This behavior was defined as the retention of at least 95% of the initial signal, and it exhibited that the proposed ECL sensor could maintain its performance up to 32 days before losing its activity (%RSD = 5.67) (Figs. S4B and SI). All things considered, it signified that this outlined ECL sensor has a satisfying stability and reproducibility. The decreased signal may be due to the loss of electrochemical properties of the electrode surface caused by oxygen in the moisture condition. Apart from, the decomposition of capture antibody may account for this decreased signal. Thereby, the finished sensor could be stored in oxygen-free environment or inert gas condition to extend its sensing capability.

The selectivity and specificity of the developed sensor were thoroughly investigated because a practical sample contains numerous types of interferences. The detection of L. monocytogenes was carried out in the presence of the nontargeting pathogens including salmonella enterica serotype enteritidis, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Vibrio parahaemolyticus, common interferences in food product. ECL signal responses of the studied interferences (1.0 × 102 CFU mL−1) and target analyte (1.0 × 102 CFU mL−1) are demonstrated in Figs. S5 and SI. It is totally obvious that the ECL signal responses either individual or mixed sample would completely distinguish in the presence of L. monocytogenes. There is no significant alteration for all studied interferences even they were tested at the highest concentration that can be possibly found in a real sample. As the results demonstrated, it can be strongly concluded that this established ECL sensing platform has high selectivity and specificity for L. monocytogenes detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Listeria monocytogenes determination in food sample
ce:section.ce:para: The three commercial food products including milk, sausage and ham were selected to demonstrate the sensing capability of the developed ECL sensor for real sample analysis. These samples were spiked with different concentrations of L. monocytogenes target and subsequently measured using this proposed platform according to the protocol earlier described. The amount of L. monocytogenes presenting in the samples was quantified by external standard method using the established curve. These obtained amounts were then compared with the standard SO 11290–2:2017 method, a horizontal method for L. monocytogenes, to confirm the accuracy and precision of this developed method. As concluded in Table 2 , the results obtained from both strategies were in good agreement, and the %RSDs and the % recoveries in the interval ranges from 0.13 to 10.4 and from 105 to 121 were attained by using this method. The created ECL sensor can quantify the certain amount of L. monocytogenes existing in real sample, which further indicated its reliability and accuracy. The promissing results proved that this ECL sensing platform is immensely suitable and might be an alternative method for rapid and sensitive detection of L. monocytogenes.

In summary, we successfully fabricate ECL sensor based on N-decorated CD synthesized by a green microwave-assisted methodology for ultrasensitive determination of L. monocytogenes. The doped nitrogen is a key procedure for ECL signal improvement. Produced via a single step microwave-assisted method, the resulted NCDs with homogenous size distribution are achieved (~4 nm). The created ECL sensor exhibits an increase in ECL signal response in the presence of L. monocytogenes target, which are approximately 5 times greater than that of the ECL responsive signal obtained from the unmodified CD. The GOOH modifier allows direct formation of the capture antibodies and enhances the electrochemical properties, future leading to a substantial enchantment of ECL intensity. K2S2O8 is proved to be an effective coreactant offering high-ECL signal response, cost-effectiveness, and less toxicity. The NCD together with K2S2O8 play a vital part to completely succeed the ECL-RET reaction. At optimal experimental conditions, this sensor exhibits wide linearity (2.0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1) and offers an extremely high sensitivity (0.104 CFU mL−1). Also, this proposed ECL sensor possesses long-term stability up to 32 days, high reproducibility, and selectivity, and has been triumphantly applied to detect L. monocytogenes in different food products with the satisfactory results.

Sakda Jampasa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Nattaya Ngamrojanavanich: Validation, Methodology. Sirirat Rengpipat: Validation, Methodology. Orawon Chailapakul: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Kurt Kalcher: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sudkate Chaiyo: Conceptualization, Visualization, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SJ and SC gratefully appreciate the supports from Second Century Fund (C2F), Chulalongkorn University. In addition, OC is also thankful for the financial support from the Ratchadapiseksompoch Endowment Fund (2016), Chulalongkorn University (CU-59-038-AM), and the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT).
